cards.text: A catch-all handler uses the syntax {{c1::catch (...)}} and will match {{c2::any type}} of exception.

cards.question: Which catch block handles a std::string exception?
cards.answer: The catch-all block catch (...) handles it.
cards.explanation: Since there's no catch block for std::string, the catch-all handler catches it.

cards.front: Can you access the exception object or its type inside a catch-all handler catch (...)?
cards.back: No, you cannot access the exception object or determine its type inside a catch-all handler. You only know that some exception was thrown.

cards.text: A catch-all handler should be placed {{c1::last}} in the sequence of catch blocks, because it will match {{c2::any exception}} and prevent later catch blocks from executing.

cards.question: What's wrong with this catch block ordering?
cards.answer: The catch-all handler is first, so the int catch block will never execute.
cards.explanation: Catch blocks are checked in order. Since catch-all matches everything, it should be last. Later catch blocks become unreachable.

cards.text: You can replace the default terminate handler using {{c1::std::set_terminate()}}, which takes a function pointer to your {{c2::custom termination handler}}.

cards.front: In what situations is std::terminate() called?
cards.back:
std::terminate() is called when:
  • An exception is thrown and not caught
  • An exception is thrown during stack unwinding (exception in destructor)
  • std::abort() or std::terminate() is explicitly called
  • A noexcept function throws an exception

cards.question: What happens when this code runs?
cards.answer: std::terminate() is called and the program terminates abnormally.
cards.explanation: The exception is thrown from main() with no catch block to handle it, resulting in std::terminate() being called.

cards.concept: When and why to use catch-all handlers
cards.explanation:
Catch-all handlers are useful for:
  • Preventing program termination from unknown exceptions
  • Logging unexpected errors
  • Cleanup before re-throwing
  • Top-level error handling (e.g., in main)
However, they provide no information about the exception type or value.

cards.front: What information can you get about an exception in a catch-all handler?
cards.back: None. A catch-all handler provides no access to the exception object, its type, or any information about it. You only know that some exception occurred.

cards.text: If an exception is thrown while another exception is being handled (during stack unwinding), {{c1::std::terminate()}} is immediately called. This is why {{c2::destructors}} should not throw.

cards.question: Why wrap main() in try-catch like this?
cards.answer: To prevent abnormal termination and allow graceful error handling with proper exit code.
cards.explanation: Catching all exceptions in main() prevents std::terminate() and allows the program to exit gracefully with an error code.
